L1.area: Application setup, configuration management, and core utility functions

L2.description: An insufficient entropy vulnerability exists in the generation of a cryptographic salt used for encryption operations. The salt is derived from a time-based, non-cryptographic function whose output is predictable. An attacker can determine the approximate generation timestamp from publicly accessible application metadata and brute-force the remaining entropy offline, enabling them to forge valid encrypted password recovery tokens and escalate privileges to an administrator account.

L3.area: Application installation configuration scripts and the core encryption utility module within the objects layer
L3.description: An insufficient entropy vulnerability exists in the generation of a cryptographic salt during the initial application installation process. The salt is produced by a standard library function that returns a time-based hexadecimal identifier rather than cryptographically random bytes. This salt is used as key material for AES-256-CBC encryption of security-sensitive tokens, including password recovery codes. An attacker can retrieve the installation timestamp from publicly accessible application metadata endpoints and brute-force the microsecond component offline, enabling them to reconstruct the salt, decrypt or forge encrypted tokens, and escalate privileges to an administrator account.

ground_truth.reason: The application generates a cryptographic salt using uniqid(), a time-based function with insufficient entropy, allowing an attacker to brute-force the salt offline and forge valid encrypted password recovery codes for privilege escalation.
